reason: Fixes meta-260606T0155Z-loop-surface-inject-spawnandcall-chicken-egg. The old code wrote the JSON-RPC initialize message inside the child.stdout.on("data") handler; since the first stdout data from a freshly spawned MCP server is the response to initialize, the send never fired and every session-start probe hit the 10s timeout. Phase 4 of plan 260605 then logged a meta_state_report finding (subtype=mcp-connection) and printed a banner — masking the underlying bug as a transient connection issue. Verified empirically 2026-06-06: before fix, hook output was the failure banner with reason=timeout; after fix, hook output is the success block (49 tools, 8 record types, 6 active findings). The new real-spawn test (loop-surface-inject-real-spawn.test.cjs) locks the end-to-end behavior by racing the probe against a 9s wall clock — the old code would have failed this test at ~9s. Closes Gap #2 (meta-260605T1356Z-sp0-sp3-tools-require-live-mcp-server-connection) empirically: the SessionStart probe now succeeds in real sessions, so the 5 SP0-SP3 tools are reachable via the canonical mcp__learning_loop_mcp__* surface.

reason: Closes the splitSegments-quote-unaware bug (finding meta-260606T0301Z-...). The P1 G8 subcommand-class fix (meta-260606T0225Z-...) shipped a refined rule pattern but assumed splitSegments handled quoted message bodies correctly. Empirically 2026-06-06, a multi-paragraph git commit describing the P1 fix itself (with trigger phrases like 'propose/design/create a new schema, new schema, create schema' inside -m bodies) was still escalated by the rule, because the ';' in 'false positives; CLI subcommand' split the body into 16 segments, and each fragment was checked independently by the regex. The new state machine keeps the entire quoted body as one segment, so stripMessageFlags correctly strips the -m value and the regex sees only 'git commit'. Blast radius: every applyPromotedRules + matchConstraintPattern caller. POSIX shell semantics preserved (single quotes are literal; double quotes allow backslash escapes; backslash outside quotes escapes the next char).

title: Meta-state cross-reference fields — typed fields on rule and loop-design schemas
description:
Schema enhancement design captured for a future plan: add formal cross-reference field for this change-log/finding targets or relates to that finding relationships. Current state (2026-06-06): meta-state.jsonl has no formal related-to or design-target field. The only formal cross-reference fields are (1) consolidated_into (finding -> change-log; valid only with status=superseded), (2) consolidates (change-log -> finding; means these findings are superseded by this change-log), and (3) supersedes (change-log -> change-log). None of these express this design note targets that active finding. Proposed shape (option A): extend change-log applies_to to include findings: applies_to.findings: z.array(z.string()).optional(). Proposed shape (option B): add a generic related_to: z.array(z.string()).optional() to both finding and change-log schemas. Use case: design notes (like the cold-session test rule design captured in meta-260606T1531Z-cold-session-test-rule-deferred, which targets meta-260606T0443Z-mcp-tools-not-loaded-into-agent-tool-list) need to formally reference the target finding they are a design for. Workaround in use today: mention the target finding id in the description field (discoverable via grep, not via schema). Status: design note; deferred for a future plan per operator decision 2026-06-06. Operators and future plans discover this design note via meta_state_list with subtype=meta-state-schema-enhancement.

GAP ADDED 2026-06-08: The original design proposed typed cross-reference fields (proposed_design_for + addresses), but did not include the CRUD operations needed to maintain those fields. The MCP tool surface covers Create, Read, and (partially) Resolve, but lacks Update/Patch on existing entries. Concrete instance: this entry was just updated to add addresses=[the CRUD-coverage finding] via direct I/O (node -e importing core/meta-state.js#updateEntry) because meta_state_propose_design returns already_exists on id collision. The gap recurs in any context where an agent needs to update an existing entry: backfilling code_fingerprint on a resolved finding, updating evidence_code_ref after a :line -> #anchor refactor, refining a rule refinement_reason, etc. The fix must ship as part of this design: meta_state_patch (new tool with CAS) OR meta_state_propose_design update-or-create mode. Once the CRUD operation exists, the cross-reference fields become operationally useful (an agent can update proposed_design_for when the design evolves, can update addresses when a new finding motivates the design) rather than declarative-only (set at creation, frozen).

description: The 2026-06-09 MCP Server Stale-Code Problem session handoff mis-described the path-resolution fix in `meta_state_refresh_tools` as joining against `root`, producing paths like `<root>/tools/gate-tool.js`. The actual fix (confirmed by direct probe in the next session, then by end-to-end MCP smoke test) is `SERVER_DIR = join(__dirname, "..")` — i.e., paths are relative to `server.js` (which lives at `<server>/`), not the project root. The followup is needed so that future agents reading prior session narratives do not re-implement the wrong fix when re-deriving the path semantics, and so that the loop's self-model has an accurate record of how the bug was actually fixed.

The TTL finding predicted that auto-resolve on expiry would make bugs disappear without trace; the closeout finding is the proof. This session (2026-06-09) only found the closeout bug because the operator explicitly asked to check it — the active set had already lost it to auto-resolve. A finding may be expired but the bug may still exist, or conversely the bug may be fixed but the finding was never properly resolved. title: meta_state_patch wire-format recursion: unwrap {item:[...]} + ZodObject passthrough recursion
description: The meta_state_patch MCP tool has two related wire-format bugs that corrupt data when an agent calls it via the MCP stdio transport: (1) top-level array values in patch get wrapped as {item: [...]} when the patch object contains other top-level fields alongside the array, and (2) coerceParamsToSchema does not recurse into ZodObject schemas with .shape missing (the passthrough case used by patch: z.object({}).passthrough()). The cross-reference-fields plan worked around (1) by patching the array alone, then scalars alone. Plan 260609-adopt-instruction-layer hit both bugs in Phase 3 and required an operator-approved node -e escape hatch to unwrap the corrupted value. Proposed fix: (a) extend coerceParamsToSchema to recurse into passthrough ZodObjects AND unwrap {item: [...]} when the target field is a ZodArray; (b) add a regression test file that calls meta_state_patch over the stdio transport with a combined patch (array + scalars) and asserts the stored value is a flat array, not a wrapped object. The structural fix (Bridge 5) is to derive the patch tool schema from the loop-design schema at startup so passthrough goes away. Until then, this design ships the symptom-level fix. Addresses 2 gaps: the meta_state_patch array wrap (newly observed) and the boolean coercion in meta_state_report (related symptom of the same wire-format root cause).

title: Meta-state refresh-fingerprint loop circuit-breaker (idempotency cache + report auto-default)
description: Two-phase fix for the meta_state_refresh_fingerprint retry-loop pathology. Phase 1: in-handler idempotency cache (Map keyed on (id, previous_code_fingerprint), 60s TTL) collapses repeat calls within the same MCP server process to a single result, with a cache_hit: true flag for observability. Phase 2: auto-default mechanism_check: true when evidence_code_ref is present in meta_state_report (using ?? not ||, so explicit mechanism_check: false is preserved), plus a structured warnings array in the response when the caller passes evidence_code_ref with explicit mechanism_check: false. Both phases together: the auto-default closes the confusing-error half (a finding with evidence_code_ref and mechanism_check: null is unreachable), and the cache caps the blast radius of any model reflex that survives (153 identical calls collapse to 1). Compound fix is required: shipping only the cache leaves a UX bug (silent miss when agents provide evidence_code_ref without mechanism_check); shipping only the auto-default leaves a structural gap (any model reflex that re-runs refresh 100+ times still wastes 53 minutes). Touchpoints: meta-state-refresh-fingerprint-tool.js (~10 lines for the cache + get/set + wire-up + export _clearCacheForTests) and meta-state-report-tool.js (~9 lines: change one spread, add 8 lines for warnings). Tests: 4 cache tests (same key returns cache_hit, different fingerprint is miss, TTL expiry re-runs, error responses also cached) + 5 report tests (default-true when evidence_code_ref provided, no warning on explicit true, warning on explicit false, no field when both absent, escape hatch when only mechanism_check provided). Plan path: plans/260610-1900-meta-state-refresh-loop-circuit-breaker/. Brainstorm: plans/reports/brainstorm-260610-1900-meta-state-refresh-loop-circuit-breaker.md. Predecessor finding meta-260606T2106Z-agent-called-meta-state-log-change-mcp-tool-5-times-in-succe resolved by auto-resolve without code fix, leaving the gap open.

title: Session-closeout audit agent (replaces ck:journal)
description: Session-closeout audit agent: before closing a session, an automated agent scans the session transcript + filesystem deltas + tool-call history for any problems that occurred but were not yet filed to meta-state (finding, change-log, or rule). For each unfiled problem, the agent auto-files the appropriate entry kind. Replaces the manual `ck:journal` mechanism (the operator-or-agent writes a journal entry at session end) with a loop-owned enforcement surface that cannot be forgotten. The motivation is the recurring pattern observed across 2026-06-06..2026-06-13: agents (including this one) ship code changes, gate fixes, and tool deletions without filing findings for the bugs they encountered en route; the next session has to rediscover the same problems (4 pre-existing test failures filed 2026-06-13, all of which had been latent for 2+ days; the L2 probe test flakiness pattern recurring 2026-06-10 and again 2026-06-13; the context-pollution-stale-workaround-language pattern where a fixed bug's workaround language persisted in AGENTS.md because the agent applying the fix did not file a follow-up). The design space: (1) hook-based: SessionEnd hook scans the transcript + gate-decision log for unfiled problems, prompts the agent before close, (2) MCP-tool-based: ck:closeout MCP tool that the agent calls before session end, (3) workflow-based: workflow_session_closeout triggered by the orchestrator, (4) ad-hoc convention: the agent is supposed to call meta_state_report on any unfiled problem (current state, often skipped). The recommended path is (1) hook-based: it is the only path that cannot be skipped, and it is the path the loop already uses for SessionStart (loop-surface-inject.cjs, H1-H14 hint injection). The hook would: (a) collect all gate-decision blocks (decision: block, decision: escalate) emitted during the session; (b) collect all TODO/HACK/FIXME/XXX comments added to the diff; (c) collect all untracked files at session end; (d) for each, check meta_state_list for an existing finding/change-log with the same evidence_code_ref; (e) if no match, file a finding with the session as session_id and the gate decision / TODO comment / untracked file as evidence. The agent is then asked to ack-or-amend the auto-filed findings before session close. This unifies the audit trail across all session activities: code changes ship with the change-log (already enforced), but the *problems* the agent encountered en route (gate blocks, TODOs left in code, untracked files that look like scratch work) get captured too. Phase: deferred to after the productization master tracker Phase A-G ships (per the operator's "much later" framing), but worth writing down now because the design space is non-trivial and the failure mode (unfiled problems haunting the next session) is already paying the loop's cognitive-load cost.

description: Import-chain analysis is the canonical dead-code detection method; keyword-based search misses transitive core dependencies. The anti-pattern has recurred 4 times across dead-code cleanup plans: (1) plan 260613-1000 missed core/candidate-to-experiment/{experiment-draft-builder,template-registry}.js + __tests__/bridge-2-unit.test.js; (2) plan 260613-1421 caught (keyword scan worked for that batch); (3) plan 260613-1530 missed core/{schema-description-loader,schema-loader,yaml-parse-wrapper}.js + scout/index.js; (4) this session caught all 7 via grep + reverse import map. Total: 11 stranded files across 2 of 4 cleanup passes. Process fix: when a tool file is deleted, run import-chain analysis on its dependencies (walk all .js files, extract import/require statements, build reverse map, find files with zero live importers excluding entry points) before declaring the cleanup complete. Keyword matching (substring search for the deleted file's basename) is deprecated for this task. The 'meta-260613T1546Z-dead-code-cleanup-process-is-keyword-based-not-import-chain' finding (now resolved) captured the same lesson; this finding elevates it to a meta-pattern. Promote to rule entry so the loop enforces the import-chain step before any tool deletion.

Gate-enforced rule: rule-import-chain-analysis-after-tool-deletion. Pattern type=regex; pattern=rm\s+[^\s]*-tool\.js|rm\s+.*tools/learning-loop-mcp/[^\s]+\.js|git\s+rm\s+[^\s]*-tool\.js. Agent-side consult: when the agent is about to delete a tool file or any .js file under tools/learning-loop-mcp/, the loop reminds the agent to run import-chain analysis (walk all .js files, extract import/require statements, build reverse map, find files with zero live importers) before declaring the cleanup complete. Keyword matching (substring search for the deleted file's basename) is deprecated for this task. Originates from finding meta-260613T1615Z which captured the 4-occurrence meta-pattern (11 stranded files across 2 of 4 cleanup plans 260613-1000, 260613-1421, 260613-1530, this session). The process fix from meta-260613T1546Z-dead-code-cleanup-process-is-keyword-based-not-import-chain is now enforced by the loop. Scope: project_has_learning_loop_mcp.

description: Cold-session discoverability test (cold-session-discoverability.test.cjs) is flaky and hard to debug. 21 meta-state entries with session_id=test-cold-session-mcp-client-loading exist (8 archived, 8 superseded, 3 resolved, 1 active, 1 reported). The test spawns a full MCP server via droid exec with 2-minute timeout, making failures slow to reproduce. Root cause of flakiness: the test depends on live MCP server behavior, wire-format coercion, and Zod schema validation — all of which can drift between test runs. The direct-MCP-server-spawn sub-test fails with invalid_union errors on meta_state_patch when the derived union schema rejects inputs that the old passthrough accepted. Recommendation: refactor the cold-session test to be deterministic (mock the MCP server or use a test harness) rather than relying on live server behavior. Alternatively, split the test into fast unit tests (tool schemas, hint assertions) and a slow integration test (droid exec) that can be skipped in CI.

description: No MCP path exists to unarchive a meta-state entry or transition out of `status: archived`. The IMMUTABLE_PATCH_FIELDS deny-list in meta-state-patch-tool.js:6-23 blocks any attempt to clear `archived_at` / `archived_by` / `archived_reason` via meta_state_patch. In session 06085a38 (transcript line 465) the recovery from an erroneous rule archival required a direct Python edit of meta-state.jsonl, which bypassed the gate/audit surface, skipped the CAS path through updateEntry, and left `resolved_at`/`resolution` fields on the now-active entry. P0/P1 fixes for the same incident shipped (archive/resolve entry-kind guards, session_id discoverability, bulk-archive preview). The recommended remediation per plans/reports/debugger-260614-0207-session-06085a38-meta-state-process-gaps.md is either (a) extend meta_state_patch to allow archived -> active transitions and auto-clear archived_* fields, or (b) add a dedicated meta_state_unarchive tool. Direct JSONL edits must not remain a recovery path.

No automated registry-consistency check exists to detect entries whose `status` and audit fields disagree. After the manual restore in session 06085a38, meta-state.jsonl:19 carried `status: active` together with `resolved_at` + `resolution` (Finding 4 of plans/reports/debugger-260614-0207-session-06085a38-meta-state-process-gaps.md). The P0 cleanup removed the visible residue, but a defense-in-depth probe is needed so the next drift is caught at the registry layer, not by an operator. Expected invariants: `status: active` must not carry `resolved_at` / `resolution`; `status: archived` must carry `archived_at` / `archived_by` / `archived_reason`; `status: resolved` must carry `resolved_by`; `status: superseded` must carry `consolidated_into`. Remediation: add a test under tools/learning-loop-mcp/__tests__/ that walks the live registry and fails on any status/audit-field mismatch, OR expose a `meta_state_consistency_check` MCP probe that returns the list of drifted entries without mutating state.

description:
The runtime-agnostic pattern is real in this codebase (shim-not-fork, protocol-adapter, cross-surface iteration) but is NOT codified as a rule. The pattern exists in the code; what's missing is the codified invariant + the surfaces that teach it to future features and audit compliance.

Two existing asymmetries surface the gap:

1. tools/learning-loop-mcp/core/gate-logic.js:407 — GLOB_SCOPE_WHITELIST = ["product/", "docs/", "plans/", "tools/", ".factory/", "meta-state.jsonl"]. The list includes ".factory/" but NOT ".claude/". A future Claude-targeted glob pattern (e.g., a Claude-coordination equivalent of rule-project-skill-boundary) would be silently dropped by the whitelist check. This is a known asymmetry between the two supported runtimes.

2. tools/learning-loop-mcp/core/inbound-state.js:34-65 — readLastOperatorMessage hard-codes both .claude/coordination/ and .factory/coordination/ paths inline. The cross-surface iteration is duplicated logic; if a third surface is added, the function needs editing in two places (lines 45-54 + 59-69). Same anti-pattern recurs in the bash gate's PATH_WRITE_PATTERNS which lists ".claude/coordination/" and ".factory/coordination/" markers separately (tools/learning-loop-mcp/hooks/bash-gate.js:31-41).

The fix is the layered design captured in plans/reports/brainstorm-260615-1400-runtime-agnostic-features-rule.md. The design authors 6 implementation artifacts:

- AGENTS.md §2 amendment (the design spec; subsection "Runtime-Agnostic Pattern" + 6-item checklist)
- Meta-state rule entry rule-runtime-agnostic-features (the codified invariant; enforcement: agent, pattern_type: consult-checklist, pattern = JSON-serialized 6-item checklist)
- New pattern type consult-checklist in core/gate-logic.js#applyPromotedRules (no-op for command-time; recognized for the rule to load; precedent: resolution-evidence-required added 2026-06-06 in meta-260606T1656Z-...)
- New MCP tool check_runtime_agnostic at tools/learning-loop-mcp/tools/check-runtime-agnostic-tool.js (the audit surface; agent-consulted; new agent-manifest group runtime_agnostic)
- core/surfaces.js helper (the cross-surface API; SURFACES = [".claude", ".factory"] is the single source of truth; helpers getAllCoordinationPaths / writeToAllSurfaces / readFromAllSurfaces)
- __tests__/runtime-agnostic.test.js (the regression guard; locks in the existing universal pattern; catches new violations at test time)

Plus 2 refactors that fix the existing asymmetries:
- core/gate-logic.js#GLOB_SCOPE_WHITELIST — refactor to use surfaces.js#SURFACES; closes the .claude/ asymmetry.
- core/inbound-state.js#readLastOperatorMessage — refactor to use surfaces.js#readFromAllSurfaces; DRY the cross-surface iteration.

The 6-item checklist (the rule's pattern):
1. core-in-universal-location — core logic in tools/learning-loop-mcp/{core,hooks,tools}/, not under .claude/ or .factory/
2. shims-in-sync — if hooks are needed, both .claude/coordination/hooks/ and .factory/coordination/hooks/ have a shim that delegates to the universal hook
3. protocol-adapter-i-o — hook stdin/stdout goes through protocol-adapter.js (parseInput, formatOutput, normalizeToolName)
4. manifest-registered — new MCP tools registered in tools/learning-loop-mcp/agent-manifest.json with group and description
5. cross-surface-iteration — code touching surface-specific paths uses the surfaces.js helper, not hard-coded .claude/ or .factory/ paths
6. parameterized-for-new-surfaces — SURFACES is the single source of truth; adding a new runtime is a 1-line append, not a refactor

Resolution criteria (all 6 must hold):
- AGENTS.md §2 has the "Runtime-Agnostic Pattern" subsection listing the 6 items
- meta-state.jsonl has rule-runtime-agnostic-features active with enforcement: agent, pattern_type: consult-checklist
- core/gate-logic.js#applyPromotedRules handles consult-checklist as a no-op (with optional debug warning)
- tools/learning-loop-mcp/tools/check-runtime-agnostic-tool.js exists, registered in agent-manifest.json runtime_agnostic group, returns structured feedback with fix_suggestion
- tools/learning-loop-mcp/core/surfaces.js exists; SURFACES is the only place runtime names are listed; helpers are tested
- tools/learning-loop-mcp/__tests__/runtime-agnostic.test.js passes (regression guard; mutation test: adding a hard-coded surface path to core/ fails the test)
- core/gate-logic.js#GLOB_SCOPE_WHITELIST uses surfaces.js#SURFACES (closes the .claude/ asymmetry)
- core/inbound-state.js#readLastOperatorMessage uses surfaces.js#readFromAllSurfaces (DRY)
- All 840+ existing tests pass; 0 regressions; new tests for all 6 artifacts

The design honors AGENTS.md §6 (Internalization Rule: cite the code, not the markdown). It is a meta-surface rule per §1: the only bound surface; the product surface is unbound and re-debated from the meta-surface. The 6-item checklist is a design-time invariant for the loop itself — the loop's self-model includes "features must be runtime-agnostic". The rule surfaces in loop_describe warm tier via the discoverability_hints mechanism. Future runtimes add themselves by appending to SURFACES; the loop doesn't auto-detect runtimes.

description: The new stripNodeEvalBody function in tools/learning-loop-mcp/core/gate-logic.js blanks the body of node -e|--eval|-p|--print wrappers before constraint-pattern regex matching. Trade-off: node -e "require('child_process').exec('npm install')" no longer matches the package-manager constraint (the command is inside the blanked body). The operator/agent can resolve this finding manually by refining the strip or by promoting a new gate rule that catches node -e wrappers specifically.

description: inbound-gate.js still contains a local TTL-based staleness check (`findStaleObservations`, lines 50-57) that duplicates the semantics of `core/inbound-state.js#checkObservationStaleness`. The old plan 260614-1856-GH-1259-fix-stale-records-references phase 2 step 5 asked to consolidate them, but that plan is completed and the step was never executed. A direct consolidation is blocked by the F1 fix from plan 260517-2300-gate-v2-staleness-fixes: the inbound gate must only write the operator-message marker when observations are actually stale, because `checkObservationStaleness` requires an existing marker to detect staleness. Needs a new design/session independent of plan 260614-1856: either a unified staleness primitive that supports both TTL bootstrap and marker-relative semantics, or a change to the F1 invariant. description:
The `meta_state_log_change` MCP tool can return `logged: true` and `cache_hit: false` without actually persisting the entry to `meta-state.jsonl` or the MCP backend's queryable state. Symptom is non-deterministic — the bug does not reproduce on every call.

**Verified 2026-06-19 (audit trail incident during PR #7 merge):**

1. Called the tool at 12:37:56Z with valid args (change_dimension: semantic, change_target: plans/reports/productization-260612-1530-master-tracker.md, change_diff: full diff, reason: ≥20 chars).
2. Tool returned `logged: true, id: meta-260619T1937Z-plans-reports-productization-260612-1530-master-tracker-md, cache_hit: false`.
3. Verified non-persistence:
   - `grep -c "meta-260619T1937Z" meta-state.jsonl` → 0
   - `tail -1 meta-state.jsonl` → last entry is `meta-260619T1320Z` (created 06:20:29Z, before the call)
   - `mcp meta_state_list({id: "meta-260619T1937Z-..."})` → `count: 0`
   - File `mtime` was updated later by an unrelated write (the file was modified at 19:47 local, 10 minutes after the call, but my entry was not appended)
4. A subsequent call at 15:29:15Z (3 hours later) with the same content but a new timestamp (id `meta-260619T2229Z-...`) persisted correctly — verified in file (1 match) and MCP backend (status=active, count=1). Required manual re-file and follow-up commit `6cd6669` on main.

**Root cause (suspected, not yet confirmed):** the handler at `tools/learning-loop-mcp/tools/meta-state-log-change-tool.js:102-113` trusts `writeEntry(root, entry)` to throw on failure. The result `{ logged: true, ... }` is constructed unconditionally after the await. If `writeEntry` returns without throwing but also without persisting (e.g., `root` resolution drift, file race with another writer, or an internal catch-and-log block in `writeEntry`), the handler cannot detect the failure. The cache hit at line 71-80 also returns the cached `logged: true` result, so even re-querying the same `(change_dimension, change_target, reason)` would falsely confirm success.

**Severity rationale:** escalate. The master tracker's "Update Protocol" (line 377) requires `meta_state_log_change` to file the audit trail; an unreliable success indicator breaks the protocol's invariant ("the tracker is canonical; the registry is the audit trail"). Operators may believe an audit entry exists when it does not, leading to silent gaps in the change-history reconstruction. PRs may merge with the expectation of a recorded change-log when none exists.

**Repro is not deterministic** but the failure mode is observable: file the change-log, then immediately grep the file for the new id. If the call came after a recent restart of the MCP server or after other registry writes, the failure rate is higher (suspected, not measured).

**What I want from this finding:**
1. Confirm the root cause (likely in `writeEntry` at `tools/learning-loop-mcp/core/meta-state.js:346` or in `resolveRoot`).
2. Add a `writeEntry` return-value assertion in the handler: treat the response as failure if the entry is not visible via `meta_state_list` immediately after the call.
3. Decide on cache semantics: the 60s idempotency cache should NOT return `logged: true` if the underlying write failed.
4. Add a regression test in `tools/learning-loop-mcp/__tests__/meta-state-log-change-tool.test.js` that mocks `writeEntry` to return without persisting and asserts the handler returns an error (not `logged: true`).

The `meta_state_report` MCP tool silently overwrites an operator-supplied `id` with an auto-generated one. The schema marks `id` as optional, but the documentation does not state that provided values are ignored. This breaks operator workflows that rely on a specific id for cross-referencing or pre-coordination with other tools (e.g., `meta_state_relationships` lookup, `meta_state_derive_status` by id, or the `reopens` field on a follow-up finding).

**Verified 2026-06-19 (same session as the silent-persistence-fail finding, filing #1):**

1. Called `meta_state_report` with explicit id: `meta-260619T2235Z-mcp-meta-state-log-change-silent-persistence-fail`
2. Tool returned: `id: meta-260619T2233Z-the-meta-state-log-change-mcp-tool-can-return-logged-true-an` (different timestamp, different slug — auto-generated from the description's first ~64 chars)
3. The provided id was not honored, not echoed in the response, and not surfaced in any error/warning field. The response shape is identical to a successful "you set the id" call, so the operator cannot tell from the response alone that the id was overwritten.
4. The auto-generated id is correct (unique, properly slugged, references the right system) — but it is not the id the operator requested.

**Why this matters:** the `id` parameter in the schema is documented as "Standard meta-state id (meta-YYMMDDTHHmmZ-slug or rule-<slug>)". It is not marked as `optional: true` with auto-gen fallback. An operator who reads the schema, provides a specific id, and gets back a different id with no signal has no way to know whether:
- (a) The id was respected but the response echoes the wrong field, or
- (b) The id was silently overwritten by the tool.

**Related:** this is the same class of issue as `meta-260619T2233Z-the-meta-state-log-change-mcp-tool-can-return-logged-true-an` (silent persistence fail). Both are "tool returns success without honoring the operator's request." Different failure modes, same root cause category: the tool's response shape is identical for success/failure-of-input-honor, so the operator cannot distinguish them.

**Severity rationale:** warning (not escalate). The audit trail is still correct — the entry is filed under the auto-generated id, which is well-formed and unique. The damage is operator confusion, not data corruption. But the pattern compounds: if a future change makes the auto-generated id collide with an existing entry, the result could be data loss.

**Repro is deterministic** (every call with an explicit id returns an auto-generated id). Steps:
1. Call `meta_state_report` with `id: "meta-YYMMDDTHHmmZ-my-explicit-id"`.
2. Inspect the response's `id` field.
3. Expected: same as the input. Actual: different (auto-generated from description).

**What I want from this finding:**
1. Fix the handler: either honor the operator-supplied id (after validation) or reject the call with a clear error.
2. Update the schema description to state explicitly: "If omitted, the id is auto-generated. Provided ids are honored verbatim if they match the pattern `meta-YYMMDDTHHmmZ-slug` and are unique."
3. Add a regression test in `tools/learning-loop-mcp/__tests__/meta-state-report-tool.test.js` that asserts the response id matches the input id when one is provided.
4. Consider a follow-up rule (`rule-mcp-tools-must-honor-explicit-input-ids` or similar) to prevent this class of issue from recurring in other write tools (`meta_state_propose_design`, `meta_state_resolve` may have the same bug).

description: The `pnpm test` glob (9 namespaces, 1100+ tests, ~13s on this dev machine) was claimed to take 10 min and buffer spec-reporter output until completion. Pre-commit stays on `pnpm test`. TaskUpdate tool-level idempotency is a separate Layer 2-general fix (out of Plan B scope).

description: When code is modified, findings anchored to file paths drift via SHA-256 hash_mismatch. The cold-tier-regression.test.js asserts ALL mechanism_check=true findings pointing to non-test .js files must be grounded. After Phase 4 modified tools/learning-loop-mastra/server.js and create-loop-tool.js, 3 findings drifted: server.js:13 (×2: original + F4 patch), create-loop-tool.js, plus 2 legacy meta-state-*-tool.js files (those are code_missing drift, test skips those). The fix is to run meta_state_refresh_fingerprint for ALL drifted findings BEFORE running the cold-tier e2e test, not after. Today the closeout ran refresh post-discovery; this should be a pre-flight step in any plan that modifies mastra/ files. Recommended fix: add a pre-closeout hook that (a) calls meta_state_query_drift, (b) refreshes each hash_mismatch fingerprint, (c) only then runs the full test suite. Without this, plans that legitimately modify code appear to break the e2e gate and require ad-hoc refresh during closeout.

But Claude Code's `SessionStart` hook (`.claude/settings.json:3-12`) only runs `recurrence-check-on-start.cjs` — it does NOT inject `discoverability_hints`. Only the Droid CLI's `.factory/hooks/loop-surface-inject.cjs:14-31` auto-injects hints at SessionStart. The plan documents this as a deferred follow-up (Plan B Open Question 3) but the gap is now structural: any new `pnpm-test-discipline`-style hint is only Droid-reachable by default. Two follow-up paths: (a) add a SessionStart hook in `.claude/settings.json` that calls `loop_describe({tier: "warm"})` and surfaces `discoverability_hints` for Claude Code; (b) document the asymmetry and accept Claude Code users must call the lookup tools explicitly. Either path is out of Plan B scope.

description: Claude Code's native `TaskUpdate` tool returns "Updated task #N" whether the status actually changed or not — the agent cannot distinguish a real status change from a no-op. This enabled a 190-call degenerate loop in session `caa56a15-2db7-4a83-9ec3-8ab26a8de2ff` on 2026-06-20: the agent called `TaskUpdate(taskId:5, status:completed)` 190 times in 150 seconds (~1.3/sec) with no intervening action because each call returned the same "Updated task #5" success response. The structural fix is upstream in Claude Code: make `TaskUpdate` return `{changed: true/false, already: <Y/N>}` so the agent can self-detect a no-op and stop. Plan B's local mitigation is a prompt-side throttle (originally proposed as `pnpm-test-discipline` hint Rule 3, dropped from the hint per Red Team H8 to avoid contradicting the operator-locked "TaskUpdate idempotency is a separate Layer 2-general fix" decision). This finding tracks the upstream structural fix. Closing this finding requires either: (a) Claude Code upstream change (out of this repo's control), or (b) a local tool that wraps TaskUpdate and returns the no-op signal (would require bypassing Claude Code's native tool). Out of Plan B scope per locked operator decision.
resolution: Plan 1a Phase 9 ships the agreed fix; acceptance gate passed.

description: `meta_state_relationships` graph is unidirectional on `reopens`. The cold-tier pass in `core/loop-introspect.js:285-330` builds the `reopens_inverse` map (parent_id → child_ids[]) and the relationships tool returns `inbound.reopened_by` on the parent. But the same tool returns `outbound: null` on the child when `entry.reopens` is set. Verified 2026-06-23 against `meta-260623T0223Z-...` (reopens=[`meta-260622T1439Z-...`]): `relationships({id: child, direction: outbound})` → `null`; `relationships({id: parent, direction: inbound})` → `{reopened_by: [child]}`. Workaround: query the parent. Fix: read `entry.reopens` directly on the child and populate `outbound.reopens` from it. **Blocker:** operator acknowledges the gap but has not invested time to resolve; deferred pending the rigorous relationship-of-records model in `plans/reports/brainstorm-260618-1538-phase-d-plan-split-report.md` (currently 'proposed' status, awaiting operator approval). description:
Code fingerprint mechanism is O(n) per cited-file change: each finding stores its own `code_fingerprint` (sha256 of the cited file at the time of last refresh). When the cited file changes, all N findings anchored to it must be re-fingerprinted and re-written. Compute cost is also O(n) per check because each grounding call re-hashes the file independently. Today the per-finding write is the dominant cost.

Symptom observed 2026-06-24 during the Phase D Plan 4 test migration followup (plan 260624-1609-phase-d-plan-4-test-migration-fix, resolved finding meta-260624T1558Z-phase-4-cutover-left-a-110-file-test-migration-gap-the-5-mcp). Fixing the test migration required editing tools/scripts/run-pnpm-test-namespaced.mjs and several files in tools/learning-loop-mastra/core/legacy/. Each edit invalidated 1-3 finding fingerprints. Resolving the cold-tier regression test (which asserts all mechanism_check=true findings are grounded) required 24 individual `meta_state_refresh_fingerprint` calls plus 11 individual `meta_state_patch` calls to repoint orphan evidence_code_ref values at files that had moved into the mastra `legacy/` subdirs. Both batches had to be re-run after each new code edit surfaced additional drift. The fan-out cost scales linearly with finding count per cited file; fine at the current ~30 active mechanism_check findings, but the loop's growth path is the wrong direction (every Plan ships more findings; the repair cost per code edit grows with the registry).

Root cause: each finding is a self-contained record that stores its own snapshot of the cited file's hash. The hash is the anchor's authoritative state. There is no shared file index that owns the hash, so updates must be applied per-record. The current design treats "evidence_code_ref + code_fingerprint" as a (path, hash) pair materialized in each finding; the right design treats it as a (path) foreign key into a shared index that owns the hash.

Resolution direction (not yet designed — proposed loop-design candidate, not filed yet): a file-index entry per unique evidence_code_ref path. Each finding's evidence_code_ref resolves to a file_path; the file index owns the current hash. The index is updated once per file change (e.g., file watcher or pre-commit hook). Grounding check becomes: lookup file index for path, compute hash, compare to index hash. Per-finding writes are eliminated; the only fan-out is in the index lookup, which is O(1). Per-code-change write cost goes from O(findings_per_file) to O(1). A migration plan would move existing findings off per-record fingerprints in place — replace each finding's `code_fingerprint` field with a reference to the new file index entry, then drop the per-record hash. Cold-tier regression test should continue to pass with the new design because the grounding check is semantically equivalent (file exists at cited path AND its current hash matches the index hash, which was the index's last-computed value).

Decision rule for resolution: a loop-design entry exists that specifies the file-index design, AND a migration plan moves at least 10 active findings off per-record fingerprints without breaking the cold-tier regression test, AND the per-code-change write cost for a representative file is reduced from O(findings_anchored_to_file) to O(1).

description: The meta_state_batch MCP tool bypasses the IMMUTABLE_PATCH_FIELDS deny-list for code_fingerprint. The op schema is passthrough() and the handler at tools/learning-loop-mastra/core/meta-state.js:486-565 does raw Object.assign(entries[idx], patch). Any caller can set code_fingerprint to a stale hash to suppress drift detection on a finding. Discovered 2026-06-25 during Phase E Plan 1 fingerprint repoint (plan 260624-2335-phase-e-foundation). The O(N)-constraint finding meta-260624T1920Z-code-fingerprint-mechanism-is-o-n-per-cited-file-change-each relies on code_fingerprint authoritativeness; this bypass is a structural weakness in the mechanism. The batch tool's `update` op should either (a) deny code_fingerprint in the handler, (b) keep code_fingerprint immutable for batch updates and require a separate meta_state_refresh_fingerprint call, or (c) explicitly document this backdoor as policy and add a regression test for it. The current state is undocumented.
resolution: Resolved by commit (pending on fix/batch-bypass-deny-list). The bypass is closed at the source: metaStateBatch in core/meta-state.js now consults IMMUTABLE_PATCH_FIELDS (moved to core from the patch tool as single source of truth) and throws immutable_field if any key in the update patch is in the deny-list. The batch's all-or-nothing rollback semantics ensure no entries are mutated when the deny-list fires. Added 2 regression tests (code_fingerprint denied + arbitrary IMMUTABLE_PATCH_FIELDS key denied). The existing repoint-fingerprints.cjs (which exploited the bypass for a one-time Phase 6 rename migration) is now marked historical — re-running it will fail with immutable_field, which is the documented correct behavior. meta_state_refresh_fingerprint continues to bypass the deny-list via the updateEntry path because it computes the hash itself (the documented pattern for legitimate refresh). See plan docs/journals/260626-fix-batch-bypass-deny-list.md for full diagnostic + verification.

This finding elevates meta_state_supersede to the same investigation class as meta_state_log_change. Decision rule for resolution: a follow-up plan investigates updateEntry + the subprocess invocation path, adds a writeEntry return-value assertion (treat as failure if the entry is not visible via meta_state_list immediately after the call), and adds a regression test that mocks updateEntry to return true without persisting and asserts the handler returns superseded: false.

The `meta_state_sweep` tool transitions mechanism_check=true findings from `active` to `stale` when their `created_at` or `acked_at` exceeds the 7d window, but does not follow up by re-verifying (back to `active`) or auto-resolving the entries. The Phase 6 invariant in `cold-tier-regression.test.js:52-64` asserts `stale mechanism_check <= 1`; the 2026-06-27 sweep produced 2 stale entries, exceeding the threshold and blocking the pre-commit hook.

**Verified 2026-06-27 (pre-commit hook failure during doc commit):**
1. `git commit` triggered `tools/scripts/run-pnpm-test-namespaced.mjs` (pre-commit gate).
2. `[mcp-tests]` glob failed with `Phase 6: sweep-success broken — 2 stale mechanism_check findings exceed threshold 1: meta-260619T2233Z-the-meta-state-log-change-mcp-tool-can-return-logged-true-an, meta-260619T2237Z-the-meta-state-report-mcp-tool-silently-overwrites-an-operat`.
3. Both findings previously had `status: "active"`, `last_verified_at: "2026-06-26T07:28:34.000Z"`, `created_at: 2026-06-19T15:33:55.408Z` and `2026-06-19T15:37:42.167Z` (8 days old), `acked_at: null`.
4. Sweep's `checkStaleness` apparently checks `created_at` against the 7d window and not `last_verified_at`. The 7d rule itself is correct per the existing age-out logic; the gap is that the sweep transitions to stale without closing the loop on entries that age out without ever being acked.

**Why this matters:**
- The Phase 6 invariant is a safety net for cold-tier discoverability. When 2 stale findings slip past, the discoverability signal weakens — operators reviewing `meta-state.jsonl` see dead entries that should either be re-verified (if the underlying code/bug is still relevant) or resolved (if the fix shipped without acking).
- The pre-commit hook blocks all commits until the invariant is restored. This is correct fail-closed behavior, but it forces a workaround (revert the registry sweep) that may lose the audit trail of what changed.
- The 2 stale findings (silent-persistence-fail and input-ignored) are real bugs that have been around 8 days. The sweep did the right thing mechanically; the gap is the absence of a follow-up prompt to the operator.

**Fix options:**
1. Sweep should emit a `meta_state_report` for each newly-stale entry, prompting operator triage. Keeps human-in-the-loop; doesn't auto-resolve potentially valid bugs. (Recommended.)
2. Sweep should auto-resolve entries that age out without ever being acked (treat as "auto-resolved by TTL" — matches the `auto-resolve: true` flag in metaStateFindingEntrySchema).
3. Raise the test threshold from 1 to 2 to acknowledge the gap. (Cheapest, but lowers the invariant.)

**Root cause location:**
- `tools/learning-loop-mastra/tools/legacy/meta-state-sweep-tool.js` — sweep handler that does not chain to follow-up report/resolve.
- `tools/learning-loop-mastra/__tests__/legacy-mcp/cold-tier-regression.test.js:52-64` — invariant that catches the gap.

**Repro is deterministic:** any sweep where ≥2 mechanism_check=true entries have `created_at > 7d ago` and `acked_at = null` will trip the invariant.
resolution: Fixed by implementing Option 1: meta_state_sweep now emits a meta_state_report follow-up for each newly-stale entry (apply path only). Follow-ups use category=stale-ref, severity=warning, affected_system=meta-state-tools, mechanism_check=false (so they don't pollute the Phase 6 threshold), and reopens=[original_id] (lineage). Dry-run stays non-mutating. New F1-F4 tests in meta-state-sweep-stale-transition.test.js verify the behavior. Change-log entry meta-260627T0103Z captures the semantic change. The 2 currently-stale entries (meta-260619T2233Z, meta-260619T2237Z) were operator-acked to pin acked_at, restoring the cold-tier invariant.
